***Babylon 5*** is an American [space opera](/wiki/Space_opera "Space opera") television series created by writer and producer [J. Michael Straczynski](/wiki/J._Michael_Straczynski "J. Michael Straczynski"), under the Babylonian Productions label, in association with Straczynski's Synthetic Worlds Ltd. and [Warner Bros. Domestic Television](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Television "Warner Bros. Television"). After the successful airing of a [test pilot movie](/wiki/Test_pilot_movie "Test pilot movie") on February 22, 1993, *[Babylon 5: The Gathering](/wiki/Babylon_5:_The_Gathering "Babylon 5: The Gathering")*, Warner Bros. commissioned the series for production in May 1993 as part of its [Prime Time Entertainment Network](/wiki/Prime_Time_Entertainment_Network "Prime Time Entertainment Network") (PTEN).[[2]](#cite_note-2) The show premiered in the United States on January 26, 1994, and ran for five 22-episode seasons.

The series follows the human military staff and alien diplomats stationed on a [space station](/wiki/Space_station "Space station"), *Babylon 5*, built in the aftermath of several major inter-species wars as a neutral ground for galactic diplomacy and trade. Major plotlines included intra-race intrigue and upheaval, inter-race wars and their aftermaths, and embroilment in a millennial cyclic conflict between ancient races. The human characters, in particular, become pivotal to the resistance against Earth's descent into [totalitarianism](/wiki/Totalitarianism "Totalitarianism").

Many episodes focused on the effect of wider events on individual characters. Episodes contained themes such as personal change, loss, oppression, corruption, and redemption.

Unusually for American broadcast television at the time of its airing, *Babylon 5* was conceived as a "novel for television" with a pre-planned five-year story arc, each episode envisioned as a "chapter".[[3]](#cite_note-3) Whereas contemporaneous television shows tended to maintain the overall status quo, confining conflicts to individual episodes, *Babylon 5* featured story arcs which spanned multiple episodes and even seasons, effecting permanent changes to the series [universe](/wiki/Babylon_5_(franchise) "Babylon 5 (franchise)").[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Tie-in novels, comic books, and short stories were also developed to play a significant [canonical](/wiki/Canon_(fiction) "Canon (fiction)") part in the overall story.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Straczynski announced plans for a reboot of the series in September 2021 in conjunction with Warner Bros. Television. An animated feature-length, direct-to-video film, *[Babylon 5: The Road Home](/wiki/Babylon_5:_The_Road_Home "Babylon 5: The Road Home")*, was released in August 2023.

## Setting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Setting")]

Main article: [Civilizations in Babylon 5](/wiki/Civilizations_in_Babylon_5 "Civilizations in Babylon 5")

The main *Babylon 5* [story arc](/wiki/Story_arc "Story arc") occurs between the years 2257 and 2262. The show depicts a future where Earth has a unified [Earth government](/wiki/Earth_Alliance_(Babylon_5)#Early_development "Earth Alliance (Babylon 5)") and has gained the technology for [faster-than-light](/wiki/Faster-than-light "Faster-than-light") travel using "jump gates", a kind of [wormhole](/wiki/Wormhole "Wormhole") technology allowing transport through the alternate dimension of [hyperspace](/wiki/Hyperspace "Hyperspace"). The Colonies within the Solar System and beyond make up the [Earth Alliance](/wiki/Earth_Alliance_(Babylon_5) "Earth Alliance (Babylon 5)"), which has established contact with other [spacefaring](/wiki/Spacefaring#Spacefaring_in_popular_culture "Spacefaring") species. Ten years before the series is set, Earth barely escaped destruction by the technologically superior [Minbari](/wiki/Minbari "Minbari"), who sought revenge after an Earth starship unwittingly killed their leader during [first contact](/wiki/First_contact_(science_fiction) "First contact (science fiction)"), only for them to surrender unexpectedly on the brink of victory. Earth has since established peaceful relationships with them and the Earth Alliance has become a significant and generally respected power within the galactic community.

Among the other species are the imperialist [Centauri](/wiki/Centauri_(Babylon_5) "Centauri (Babylon 5)"); the [Narn](/wiki/Narn "Narn"), who only recently gained independence from the Centauri empire; and the mysterious, powerful [Vorlons](/wiki/Vorlon "Vorlon"). Several dozen less powerful species from the [League of Non-Aligned Worlds](/wiki/League_of_Non-Aligned_Worlds "League of Non-Aligned Worlds") also have diplomatic contact with the major races, including the [Drazi](/wiki/Drazi "Drazi"), [Brakiri](/wiki/Brakiri "Brakiri"), [Vree](/wiki/Vree "Vree"), [Markab](/wiki/Civilizations_in_Babylon_5#Markab "Civilizations in Babylon 5"), and [pak'ma'ra](/wiki/Pak%27ma%27ra "Pak'ma'ra"). An ancient and secretive race, the [Shadows](/wiki/Shadow_(Babylon_5) "Shadow (Babylon 5)"), unknown to humans but documented in many other races' religious texts, malevolently influence events to bring chaos and war among the known species. Among the chaos the Shadows cause is a Centauri descent into [irredentism](/wiki/Irredentism "Irredentism") and Earth sliding into [totalitarianism](/wiki/Totalitarianism "Totalitarianism") under President Morgan Clark.

The *Babylon 5* space station is located in the [Epsilon Eridani](/wiki/Epsilon_Eridani "Epsilon Eridani") system, at the [fifth Lagrangian point](/wiki/Trojan_(celestial_body) "Trojan (celestial body)") of the fictional planet Epsilon III and its moon.[[7]](#cite_note-7) It is an [O'Neill cylinder](/wiki/O%27Neill_cylinder "O'Neill cylinder") 5 miles (8.0 km) long and 0.5–1.0 mile (0.80–1.61 km) in diameter. The station is the last of its line; the first three stations were all destroyed during construction, while *Babylon 4* was completed but mysteriously vanished shortly after being made operational.[[8]](#cite_note-4thSeason-8) It contains living areas which accommodate various alien species, providing differing atmospheres and gravities. Human visitors to the alien sectors are shown using breathing equipment and other measures to tolerate the conditions.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The five seasons of the series each correspond to one fictional sequential year in the period 2258–2262. Each season shares its title with an episode that is central to that season's plot.

### Pilot film (1993)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Pilot film (1993)")]

In the pilot film, *[Babylon 5: The Gathering](/wiki/Babylon_5:_The_Gathering "Babylon 5: The Gathering")*, the Vorlon ambassador Kosh is nearly killed by an assassin shortly after arriving at the station. Jeffrey Sinclair, the commander of *Babylon 5*, is named as the prime suspect, but is proven to have been framed.

### Season 1: Signs and Portents (1994)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Season 1: Signs and Portents (1994)")]

Commander Sinclair, a hero of the Minbari war, is troubled by his inability to remember events of the war's last day. Though supported by Minbari ambassador Delenn, who is secretly a member of the Minbari ruling Grey Council, other Minbari remain distrustful of him. The Narn ambassador G'Kar continually presses for concessions from their former overlords the Centauri Republic. Centauri ambassador Londo Mollari finds a new ally in the enigmatic Mr. Morden to strike back at the Narn. Meanwhile, xenophobic groups on Earth challenge humanity's tolerance of aliens. This tension culminates in the assassination of Earth's President Santiago, who favored such contact.

### Season 2: The Coming of Shadows (1994–1995)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Season 2: The Coming of Shadows (1994–1995)")]

Sinclair is transferred to be ambassador to Minbar, and General Hague assigns captain John Sheridan command of the station. Hague and Sheridan believe now-president Clark conspired in Santiago's death but have no proof. Clark gradually moves Earth in an isolationist direction and takes steps to install a totalitarian government. When the aging Centauri Emperor Turhan dies, Mollari and his ally Lord Refa install Turhan's unstable nephew Cartagia as emperor and force a war against the Narn. Aided by Mr. Morden's "associates" the Shadows, the Centauri decimate the Narn. The war ends with a planetary bombardment of the Narn homeworld, followed by the enslavement of the surviving Narns. Delenn and Vorlon ambassador Kosh request Sheridan's help to fight against their ancient foe, the Shadows.

### Season 3: Point of No Return (1995–1996)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Season 3: Point of No Return (1995–1996)")]

Sheridan and Delenn establish a "conspiracy of light" to fight the influence of the Shadows. When Clark declares martial law, Sheridan declares *Babylon 5*'s independence from the Earth government. Mollari realizes his deal with Mr. Morden has become dangerous but is unable to end it. As the Shadows cause conflict and chaos throughout the galaxy, Sheridan confronts Kosh and successfully convinces the Vorlons to provide military assistance against them. In retaliation for Vorlon intervention, the Shadows assassinate Kosh. Sinclair travels back in time a thousand years to aid the Minbari in the previous Shadow War, becoming the legendary Minbari religious leader Valen. Sheridan discovers vulnerabilities in the Shadow vessels and learns to predict their objectives, leading to the first major military defeat of the Shadows. Despite Kosh's warnings, Sheridan confronts the Shadows on their homeworld Z'ha'dum. He crashes a spacecraft packed with nuclear weapons into the planet, seemingly dying in the explosion.

### Season 4: No Surrender, No Retreat (1996–1997)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Season 4: No Surrender, No Retreat (1996–1997)")]

Sheridan is rescued from Z'ha'dum by the mysterious Lorien. With the Shadows in retreat, the Vorlons begin destroying any planet allied with or influenced by the Shadows. Mollari overthrows the mad emperor Cartagia with the aid of G'Kar in exchange for the liberation of the Narn from Centauri rule. Mollari betrays the Shadows in order to save the Centauri homeworld from the Vorlons. Sheridan realizes the Vorlons and Shadows have used the younger races in a [proxy war](/wiki/Proxy_war "Proxy war"), and convinces both sides to permanently end their conflict and to leave the younger races alone in peace. Sheridan next refocuses on returning democracy to Earth. He forges a new Interstellar Alliance along with the Minbari, Centauri, and Narn governments. With their help, Sheridan is able to win the Earth civil war and forces President Clark out of office. Sheridan is forced to resign from the Earth military, but is named president of the Interstellar Alliance.

### Season 5: The Wheel of Fire (1998)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Season 5: The Wheel of Fire (1998)")]

An ex-wife of Sheridan's, Elizabeth Lochley, is assigned to command the station. A group of rogue human telepaths take sanctuary on the station, seeking Sheridan's aid to escape the control of Psi Corps, the autocratic Earth agency that oversees telepaths. The Interstellar Alliance refuses to grant them a planet of their own, and they are eventually expelled from the station. Meanwhile, the Drakh, former supporters of the Shadows, seek revenge for the Shadows' defeat. They infiltrate the Centauri government and orchestrate attacks against other Alliance members. Mollari attempts to purge the alien manipulation of his government but is too late. After a devastating attack by Alliance forces on Centauri Prime, Mollari is installed as emperor, but under Drakh control. He then withdraws the Centauri from the Interstellar Alliance. Twenty years later, Sheridan has a last reunion with his friends before leaving to join Lorien and the older races "beyond the rim".

### Spin-offs and television movies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Spin-offs and television movies")]

Main article: [Babylon 5 (franchise)](/wiki/Babylon_5_(franchise) "Babylon 5 (franchise)")

The original show spawned a multimedia franchise of spin-offs consisting of a miniseries, five television movies, twenty-two novels, two tabletop games (an RPG and a wargame), and various other media such as technical books, comics, and trading cards.

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Production")]

### Origin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Origin")]

Having worked on a number of television science fiction shows which had regularly gone over budget, Straczynski concluded that a lack of long-term planning was to blame, and set about looking at ways in which a series could be done responsibly. Taking note of the lessons of mainstream television, which brought stories to a centralized location such as a hospital, police station, or law office, he decided that instead of "[going] in search of new worlds, building them anew each week", a fixed space station setting would keep costs at a reasonable level. A fan of sagas such as the [*Foundation* series](/wiki/Foundation_(book_series) "Foundation (book series)"), *[Childhood's End](/wiki/Childhood%27s_End "Childhood's End")*, *[The Lord of the Rings](/wiki/The_Lord_of_the_Rings "The Lord of the Rings")*, *[Dune](/wiki/Dune_(franchise) "Dune (franchise)")* and the [*Lensman* series](/wiki/Lensman_series "Lensman series"), Straczynski wondered why no one had done a television series with the same epic sweep, and concurrently with the first idea started developing the concept for a vastly ambitious epic covering massive battles and other universe-changing events. Realizing that both the fixed-locale series and the epic could be done in a single series, he began to sketch the initial outline of what would become *Babylon 5*.[[13]](#cite_note-GEnie-1992-17)[[14]](#cite_note-18)

> Once I had the locale, I began to populate it with characters, and sketch out directions that might be interesting. I dragged out my notes on religion, philosophy, history, sociology, psychology, science (the ones that didn't make my head explode), and started stitching together a crazy quilt pattern that eventually formed a picture. Once I had that picture in my head, once I knew what the major theme was, the rest fell into place. All at once, I saw the full five-year story in a flash, and I frantically began scribbling down notes.

—J. Michael Straczynski, 1995[[15]](#cite_note-19)

Straczynski set five goals for *Babylon 5*. He said that the show "would have to be good science fiction". It would also have to be good television, "and rarely are SF shows both good SF \*and\* good TV; there're [*[sic](/wiki/Sic "Sic")*] generally one or the other." It would have to do for science fiction television what *[Hill Street Blues](/wiki/Hill_Street_Blues "Hill Street Blues")* had done for [police dramas](/wiki/Police_drama "Police drama"), by taking an adult approach to the subject. It would have to be reasonably budgeted, and "it would have to look unlike anything ever seen before on TV, presenting individual stories against a much broader canvas." He further stressed that his approach was "to take [science fiction] seriously, to build characters for grown-ups, to incorporate real science but keep the characters at the center of the story."[[16]](#cite_note-io9_evolution-20)[[17]](#cite_note-GEnie-1991-21) Some of the staples of television science fiction were also out of the question (the show would have "no kids or cute robots").[[18]](#cite_note-22) The idea was not to present a perfect utopian future, but one with greed and homelessness; one where characters grow, develop, live, and die; one where not everything was the same at the end of the day's events. Citing [Mark Twain](/wiki/Mark_Twain "Mark Twain") as an influence, Straczynski said he wanted the show to be a mirror to the real world and to covertly teach.[[13]](#cite_note-GEnie-1992-17)

Following production on *[Captain Power and the Soldiers of the Future](/wiki/Captain_Power_and_the_Soldiers_of_the_Future "Captain Power and the Soldiers of the Future")*, Straczynski approached John Copeland and Doug Netter, who had also been involved with *Captain Power* and showed him the [bible](/wiki/Bible_(screenwriting) "Bible (screenwriting)") and pilot script for his show, and both were impressed with his ideas.[[19]](#cite_note-sonq-23) They were able to secure an order for the pilot from [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures") who were looking at the time to get programming for a planned broadcast network. Warner Bros. had remained skeptical about the show even after greenlighting the pilot. According to Straczynski, Warner Bros. had three main concerns: that American attention spans were too short for a series-long narrative to work, that it would be difficult to sell the show into [syndication](/wiki/Broadcast_syndication "Broadcast syndication") as the syndicate networks would air the episodes out of order, and that no other science-fiction television show outside of *Star Trek* had gone more than three seasons before being canceled.[[20]](#cite_note-newsarama_2019-24) Straczynski had proved out that the syndication fear was incorrect, since syndicate stations told him they show their shows in episode order to track broadcasts for royalties; however, he could not assure Warner Bros. about the attention span or premature cancellation concerns, but still set out to show Warner Bros. they were wrong.[[20]](#cite_note-newsarama_2019-24)

### Writing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Writing")]

Straczynski wrote 92 of the 110 episodes of *Babylon 5*, including all 44 episodes in the third and fourth seasons,[[21]](#cite_note-25) a feat never before accomplished in American television.[[22]](#cite_note-26) Other writers to have contributed scripts to the show include [Peter David](/wiki/Peter_David "Peter David"), [Neil Gaiman](/wiki/Neil_Gaiman "Neil Gaiman"), [Kathryn M. Drennan](/wiki/Kathryn_M._Drennan "Kathryn M. Drennan"), [Lawrence G. DiTillio](/wiki/Larry_DiTillio "Larry DiTillio"), [D. C. Fontana](/wiki/D._C._Fontana "D. C. Fontana"), and [David Gerrold](/wiki/David_Gerrold "David Gerrold"). [Harlan Ellison](/wiki/Harlan_Ellison "Harlan Ellison"), a creative consultant on the show, received story credits for two episodes.[[23]](#cite_note-27) Each writer was informed of the overarching storyline, enabling the show to be produced consistently under-budget. The rules of production were strict; scripts were written six episodes in advance, and changes could not be made once production had started.[[24]](#cite_note-28)

With not all cast members being hired for every episode of a season, the five-year plot length caused some planning difficulties. If a critical scene involving an actor not hired for every episode had to be moved, that actor had to be paid for work on an extra episode.[[20]](#cite_note-newsarama_2019-24) It was sometimes necessary to adjust the plotline to accommodate external influences, an example being the "trap door" that was written for every character: in the event of that actor's unexpected departure from the series, the character could be written out with minimal impact on the storyline.[[25]](#cite_note-29) Straczynski stated, "As a writer, doing a long-term story, it'd be dangerous and short-sighted for me to construct the story without trap doors for every single character. ... That was one of the big risks going into a long-term storyline which I considered long in advance;..."[[26]](#cite_note-30) This device was eventually used to facilitate the departures of Claudia Christian and Andrea Thompson from the series.

> First thing I did was to flip out the stand-alones, which traditionally have taken up the first 6 or so episodes of each season; between two years, that's 12 episodes, over half a season right there. Then you would usually get a fair number of additional stand-alones scattered across the course of the season. So figure another 3–4 per season, say 8, that's 20 out of 44. So now you're left with basically 24 episodes to fill out the main arc of the story.

—J. Michael Straczynski, 1996[[27]](#cite_note-writing-31)

Straczynski purposely went light on elements of the five-year narrative during the first season as he felt the audience would not be ready for the full narrative at that time, but he still managed to drop in some scenes that would be critical to the future narrative. This also made it challenging for the actors to understand their motivations without knowing where their characters were going; Straczynski said "I didn't want to tell them too much, because that risks having them play the result, rather than the process."[[20]](#cite_note-newsarama_2019-24) He recalled that Peter Jurasik had asked him about the context of Londo's premonition, shown partially in "[Midnight on the Firing Line](/wiki/List_of_Babylon_5_episodes#ep1 "List of Babylon 5 episodes")", of himself and G'Kar choking each other to death, but Straczynski had to be coy about it.[[20]](#cite_note-newsarama_2019-24) The full death scene was shown in context in "[War Without End - Part 2](/wiki/List_of_Babylon_5_episodes#ep61 "List of Babylon 5 episodes")" near the end of the third season.

During production of the fourth season, the [Prime Time Entertainment Network](/wiki/Prime_Time_Entertainment_Network "Prime Time Entertainment Network"), which Warner Bros. opted to use for *Babylon 5*, was shut down, leaving the planned fifth season in doubt. Unwilling to short-change fans of the show, Straczynski began preparing modifications to the fourth season that would allow him to conclude his overall arc should a fifth season not be greenlit, which ultimately became the direction the fourth season took. Straczynski identified three primary [narrative threads](/wiki/Narrative_thread "Narrative thread") which would require resolution: the Shadow war, Earth's slide into a dictatorship, and a series of sub-threads which branched off from those. Estimating they would still take around 27 episodes to resolve without having the season feel rushed, the solution came when the [TNT](/wiki/TNT_(American_TV_network) "TNT (American TV network)") network commissioned two *Babylon 5* television films. Several hours of material was thus able to be moved into the films, including a three-episode arc which would deal with the background to the [Earth–Minbari War](/wiki/Earth%E2%80%93Minbari_War "Earth–Minbari War"), and a sub-thread which would have set up the sequel series, *Crusade*. Further standalone episodes and plot-threads were dropped from season four, which could be inserted into *Crusade*, or the fifth season, were it to be given the [greenlight](/wiki/Greenlight "Greenlight").[[28]](#cite_note-32) The intended series finale, "[Sleeping in Light](/wiki/Sleeping_in_Light "Sleeping in Light")", was filmed during season four as a precaution against cancellation. When word came that TNT had picked up *Babylon 5*, this was moved to the end of season five and replaced with a newly filmed season four finale, "[The Deconstruction of Falling Stars](/wiki/The_Deconstruction_of_Falling_Stars "The Deconstruction of Falling Stars")".[[29]](#cite_note-33)

### Costume

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Costume")]

Ann Bruice Aling was costume designer for the show, after [production designer](/wiki/Production_designer "Production designer") John Iacovelli suggested her for the position, having previously worked with her on a number of film and theatrical productions.[[30]](#cite_note-Iacovelli3-34)

With the variety of costumes required she compared *Babylon 5* to "eclectic theatre", with fewer rules about period, line, shape and textures having to be adhered to.[[31]](#cite_note-Bruice2-35) Preferring natural materials whenever possible, such as [ostrich leather](/wiki/Ostrich_leather "Ostrich leather") in the [Narn](/wiki/Narn "Narn") body armor, Bruice combined and layered fabrics as diverse as rayon and silk with [brocades](/wiki/Brocade "Brocade") from the 1930s and '40s to give the clothing the appearance of having evolved within different cultures.[[32]](#cite_note-Bruice1-36)[[33]](#cite_note-Kim1-37)

> Often we try to coordinate the sensibilities of the aliens. I try to work with Optic Nerve to ensure that the head meets the body in some sensible way. We talk about similar qualities, textures and colors and the flow of the total being. Truthfully, often the look of the prosthetic comes somewhat earlier and from that I have an understanding of what direction to go.

— Ann Bruice Aling, 1995[[31]](#cite_note-Bruice2-35)

With an interest in costume history, she initially worked closely with Straczynski to get a sense of the historical perspective of the major alien races, "so I knew if they were a peaceful people or a warring people, cold climate etc. and then I would interpret what kind of sensibility that called for."[[32]](#cite_note-Bruice1-36) Collaborating with other departments to establish co-ordinated visual themes for each race, a broad palette of colors was developed with Iacovelli, which he referred to as "spicy brights".[[34]](#cite_note-Iacovelli1-38) These warm shades of gray and secondary colors, such as certain blues for the [Minbari](/wiki/Minbari "Minbari"), would often be included when designing both the costumes and relevant sets. As the main characters evolved, Bruice referred back to Straczynski and producer John Copeland who she viewed as "surprisingly more accessible to me as advisors than other producers and directors", so the costumes could reflect these changes. Ambassador [Londo Mollari](/wiki/Londo_Mollari "Londo Mollari")'s purple coat became dark blue and more tailored while his waistcoats became less patterned and brightly colored as Bruice felt "Londo has evolved in my mind from a buffoonish character to one who has become more serious and darker."[[31]](#cite_note-Bruice2-35)

Normally there were three changes of costume for the primary actors; one for on set, one for the stunt double and one on standby in case of "coffee spills". For human civilians, garments were generally purchased [off-the-rack](/wiki/Ready-to-wear "Ready-to-wear") and altered in various ways, such as removing lapels from jackets and shirts while rearranging [closures](/wiki/Hook-and-eye_closure "Hook-and-eye closure"), to suggest future fashions. For some of the main female characters a more [couture](/wiki/Haute_couture "Haute couture") approach was taken, as in the suits worn by [Talia Winters](/wiki/Talia_Winters "Talia Winters"), which Bruice described as being designed and fitted to within "an inch of their life". Costumes for the destitute residents of downbelow would be distressed through a combination of bleaching, sanding, dipping in dye baths and having stage blood added.[[32]](#cite_note-Bruice1-36)

Like many of the crew on the show, members of the costume department made onscreen [cameos](/wiki/Cameo_appearance "Cameo appearance"). During the season 4 episode "[Atonement](/wiki/Atonement_(Babylon_5) "Atonement (Babylon 5)")", the tailors and costume supervisor appeared as the Minbari women fitting [Zack Allan](/wiki/Zack_Allan "Zack Allan") for his new uniform as the recently promoted head of security. His complaints, and the subsequent stabbing of him with a needle by costume supervisor Kim Holly, was a light-hearted reference to the previous security uniforms, a design carried over from the pilot movie, which was difficult to work with and wear due to the combination of leather and wool.[[33]](#cite_note-Kim1-37)

### Prosthetic makeup and animatronics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Prosthetic makeup and animatronics")]

While the original pilot film featured some [aliens](/wiki/Extraterrestrial_life "Extraterrestrial life") which were puppets and [animatronics](/wiki/Animatronics "Animatronics"), the decision was made early on in the show's production to portray most alien species as [humanoid](/wiki/Humanoid "Humanoid") in appearance. Barring isolated appearances, fully [computer-generated](/wiki/Computer-generated_imagery "Computer-generated imagery") aliens were discounted as an idea due to the "massive [rendering](/wiki/Rendering_(computer_graphics) "Rendering (computer graphics)") power" required. Long-term use of puppets and animatronics was also discounted, as Straczynski believed they would not be able to convey "real emotion" without an actor inside.[[35]](#cite_note-39)

### Visuals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Visuals")]

See also: [Babylon 5 § Mastering problems](#Mastering_problems)

In anticipation of the emerging [HDTV](/wiki/HDTV "HDTV") standard, rather than the usual [4:3](/wiki/4:3 "4:3") format, the series was shot in [16:9](/wiki/16:9 "16:9"), with the image cropped to 4:3 for initial television transmissions.[[36]](#cite_note-40) It was one of the first television shows to use computer technology in creating [visual effects](/wiki/Visual_effects "Visual effects"), rather than models and miniatures, primarily out of budgetary concerns; Straczynski estimated that each of their episodes cost US$650,000 to make, compared to the US$1.5 million cost of each episode of *[Star Trek: The Next Generation](/wiki/Star_Trek:_The_Next_Generation "Star Trek: The Next Generation")*.[[20]](#cite_note-newsarama_2019-24) The visual effects were achieved using [Amiga](/wiki/Amiga "Amiga")-based [Video Toasters](/wiki/Video_Toaster "Video Toaster") at first, and later [Pentium](/wiki/Pentium_compatible_processor "Pentium compatible processor"), [Macintosh](/wiki/Macintosh "Macintosh"), and [Alpha](/wiki/DEC_Alpha "DEC Alpha")-based systems using [LightWave 3D](/wiki/LightWave_3D "LightWave 3D").[[37]](#cite_note-41)[[38]](#cite_note-42) The effects sequences were designed to simulate [Newtonian physics](/wiki/Newtonian_physics "Newtonian physics"), with particular emphasis on the effects of inertia on the motion of spacecraft.[[39]](#cite_note-43)

[Foundation Imaging](/wiki/Foundation_Imaging "Foundation Imaging") provided the special effects for the pilot film (for which it won an Emmy) and the first three seasons of the show, led by [Ron Thornton](/wiki/Ron_Thornton_(visual_effects_designer) "Ron Thornton (visual effects designer)"). After co-executive producer Douglas Netter and producer John Copeland approached Straczynski with the idea of producing the effects in-house, Straczynski agreed to replace Foundation, for season 4 and 5, once a new team had been established by [Netter Digital](/wiki/Netter_Digital "Netter Digital"), and an equal level of quality was assured,[[40]](#cite_note-44) by using similar technology and a number of former Foundation employees.[[41]](#cite_note-45) The Emmy-winning alien make-up was provided by Optic Nerve Studios.[[42]](#cite_note-46)

[Christopher Franke](/wiki/Christopher_Franke "Christopher Franke") [composed](/wiki/Musical_composition "Musical composition") and [scored](/wiki/Sheet_music "Sheet music") the musical soundtrack for all five years of the show when [Stewart Copeland](/wiki/Stewart_Copeland "Stewart Copeland"), who worked on the original telefilm, was unable to return for the first season due to recording and touring commitments.[[44]](#cite_note-48)

Initially concerned composing for an episodic television show could become "annoying because of the repetition", Franke found the evolving characters and story of *Babylon 5* afforded him the opportunity to continually take new directions.[[45]](#cite_note-ChrisFranke1-49)[[46]](#cite_note-ChrisFranke2-50) Given creative freedom by the producers, Franke also [orchestrated](/wiki/Orchestration#Film_orchestration "Orchestration") and [mixed](/wiki/Audio_mixing_(recorded_music) "Audio mixing (recorded music)") all the music, which one reviewer described as having "added another dimension of mystery, suspense, and excitement to the show, with an easily distinguishable character that separates 'Babylon 5 from other sci-fi television entries of the era."[[45]](#cite_note-ChrisFranke1-49)[[47]](#cite_note-51)

With his recording studio in the same building as his home located in the [Hollywood Hills](/wiki/Hollywood_Hills "Hollywood Hills"), Franke would attend creative meetings before scoring the 25 minutes or so of music for each episode.[[45]](#cite_note-ChrisFranke1-49) Using the "acoustic dirt produced by live instruments and the ability to play so well between two semitones" and the "frequency range, dynamics and control" provided by synthesizers, he described his approach "as experimental friendly as possible without leaving the happy marriage between the orchestral and electronic sounds".[[46]](#cite_note-ChrisFranke2-50)

Using [Cubase](/wiki/Steinberg_Cubase "Steinberg Cubase") software through an [electronic keyboard](/wiki/Electronic_keyboard "Electronic keyboard"), or for more complex pieces a [light pen](/wiki/Light_pen "Light pen") and [graphics tablet](/wiki/Graphics_tablet "Graphics tablet"), he would begin by developing the [melodic](/wiki/Melody "Melody") content round which the [ambient](/wiki/Ambient_music "Ambient music") components and [transitions](/wiki/Transition_(music) "Transition (music)") were added. Using playbacks with digital [samples](/wiki/Sampling_(music) "Sampling (music)") of the appropriate instruments, such as a group of violins, he would decide which tracks to produce electronically or record [acoustically](/wiki/Acoustic_music "Acoustic music").[[45]](#cite_note-ChrisFranke1-49)[[46]](#cite_note-ChrisFranke2-50)

Scores for the acoustic tracks were emailed to his Berlin scoring stage, and would require from four musicians to the full orchestra, with a maximum of 24 present at any one time. One of three conductors would also be required for any score that involved more than six musicians. Franke would direct recording sessions via six fiber optic digital telephone lines to transmit and receive video, music and the [SMPTE timecode](/wiki/SMPTE_timecode "SMPTE timecode"). The final edit and mixing of the tracks would take place in his Los Angeles studio.

A total of 24 episode and three television film soundtracks were released under Franke's record label, Sonic Images Records, between 1995 and 2001. These contain the musical scores in the same chronological order as they played in the corresponding episodes, or television films. Three compilation albums were also produced, containing extensively re-orchestrated and remixed musical passages taken from throughout the series to create more elaborate suites. In 2007, his soundtrack for [The Lost Tales](/wiki/Babylon_5:_The_Lost_Tales "Babylon 5: The Lost Tales") was released under the [Varèse Sarabande](/wiki/Var%C3%A8se_Sarabande "Varèse Sarabande") record label.

### Broadcast history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Broadcast history")]

Warner Bros. slotted the show to premiere on its nascent [Prime Time Entertainment Network](/wiki/Prime_Time_Entertainment_Network "Prime Time Entertainment Network") (PTEN). As original content from another studio, it was somewhat anomalous in a stable of syndicated content from Warner Bros. and the cause of some friction between Straczynski's company and Warner Bros.[[20]](#cite_note-newsarama_2019-24)

The pilot film, [*The Gathering*](/wiki/Babylon_5:_The_Gathering "Babylon 5: The Gathering"), premiered on February 22, 1993, with strong viewing figures, achieving a 9.7 in the [Nielsen](/wiki/Nielsen_ratings "Nielsen ratings") national syndication rankings.[[48]](#cite_note-benson-52) The regular series initially aired from January 26, 1994 through November 25, 1998,[[49]](#cite_note-53) first on PTEN, then in [first-run syndication](/wiki/Broadcast_syndication#First-run_syndication_in_the_U.S. "Broadcast syndication"), debuting with a 6.8 rating/10 share. Figures dipped in its second week, and while it posted a solid 5.0 rating/8 share, with an increase in several major markets,[[50]](#cite_note-54) ratings for the first season continued to fall, to a low of 3.4 during [reruns](/wiki/Rerun "Rerun").[[51]](#cite_note-55) Ratings remained low-to-middling throughout the first four seasons,[[52]](#cite_note-56) but *Babylon 5* scored well with the [demographics](/wiki/Demographics "Demographics") required to attract the leading national sponsors and saved up to $300,000 per episode by shooting off the studio lot,[[48]](#cite_note-benson-52) therefore remaining profitable.[[53]](#cite_note-57) The fifth season, which aired on cable network [TNT](/wiki/TNT_(American_TV_network) "TNT (American TV network)"), had ratings about 1.0% lower than seasons two through four.

In the United Kingdom, the show aired every week on [Channel 4](/wiki/Channel_4 "Channel 4") without a break, with the result that the last four or five episodes of the early seasons screened in the UK before the US.[[54]](#cite_note-58) *Babylon 5* was one of the better-rated US television shows on Channel 4,[[55]](#cite_note-59) and achieved high audience [Appreciation Indexes](/wiki/Appreciation_Index "Appreciation Index"), with the season 4 episode ["Endgame](/wiki/Endgame_(Babylon_5) "Endgame (Babylon 5)")" achieving the rare feat of beating the prime-time soap operas for first position.[[56]](#cite_note-60)

Straczynski stated that PTEN only required the show to be profitable for the network to remain in production, and said that while this was the case for its first four seasons, on paper it was always losing money; he also remarked in a 2019 interview that in terms of [contractual profit definition](/wiki/Hollywood_accounting "Hollywood accounting") the show remained about US$30 million in the red on paper, and stated that he had therefore never made any profits on *Babylon 5*.[[20]](#cite_note-newsarama_2019-24) The entire series cost an estimated $90 million for 110 episodes.[[57]](#cite_note-aicn1-61)

*Babylon 5* completed its five-year story arc on November 25, 1998, after five seasons and 109 aired episodes, when TNT aired the 110th ([epilogue](/wiki/Epilogue "Epilogue")) episode "[Sleeping in Light](/wiki/Sleeping_in_Light "Sleeping in Light")," which had been filmed as the Season 4 finale, when *Babylon 5* was under threat of being cut. After a fifth season was assured, a new Season 4 finale was used so that "[Sleeping in Light](/wiki/Sleeping_in_Light "Sleeping in Light")" could remain as the series finale.

### Remastered version

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Remastered version")]

In November 2020, a remastered version of the show in 4:3 format was released to the iTunes Store and Amazon Prime Video.[[58]](#cite_note-62) This version uses the original negatives for filmed elements, and algorithmically upscales the digitally created elements to HD resolution with fewer visual artifacts, for a more visually consistent presentation. In January 2021, it was made available for streaming on [HBO Max](/wiki/HBO_Max "HBO Max").[[59]](#cite_note-63)
In February 2023, HBO's license expired and streaming rights were acquired by the free streaming service Tubi.[[60]](#cite_note-64)

## Themes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Themes")]

Throughout its run, *Babylon 5* found ways to portray [themes](/wiki/Theme_(literature) "Theme (literature)") relevant to modern and historical social issues. It marked several firsts in television science fiction, including the exploration of the political and social landscapes of the first human colonies, their interactions with Earth, and the underlying tensions.[[61]](#cite_note-mars-65) *Babylon 5* was also one of the first television science fiction shows to [denotatively](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/denotative "wikt:denotative") refer to a [same-sex relationship](/wiki/Same-sex_relationship "Same-sex relationship").[[62]](#cite_note-dyke1-66)[[63]](#cite_note-67) In the show, [sexual orientation](/wiki/Sexual_orientation "Sexual orientation") is as much of an issue as "being left-handed or right-handed".[[64]](#cite_note-68) [Unrequited love](/wiki/Unrequited_love "Unrequited love") is explored as a source of pain for the characters, though not all the relationships end unhappily.[[65]](#cite_note-69)

### Order vs. chaos; authoritarianism vs. free will

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Order vs. chaos; authoritarianism vs. free will")]

> Neither the Vorlons nor the Shadows saw themselves as conquerors or adversaries. Both believed they were doing what was right for us. And like any possessive parent, they'll keep on believing that until the kid is strong enough to stand up and say, 'No, this is what ***I*** want.'

—[J. Michael Straczynski](/wiki/J._Michael_Straczynski "J. Michael Straczynski"), 1997[[66]](#cite_note-js-70)

The clash between order and chaos, and the people caught in between, plays an important role in *Babylon 5*. The conflict between two unimaginably powerful older races, the [Vorlons](/wiki/Vorlon "Vorlon") and the [Shadows](/wiki/Shadow_(Babylon_5) "Shadow (Babylon 5)"), is represented as a battle between competing ideologies, each seeking to turn the humans and the other younger races to their beliefs. The Vorlons represent an [authoritarian](/wiki/Authoritarian "Authoritarian") philosophy of unquestioning obedience. Vorlon characters frequently ask, "who are you?" focusing on identity as a catalyst for shaping personal goals;[[67]](#cite_note-71)[[68]](#cite_note-signs-72) the intention is not to solicit a correct answer, but to "tear down the artifices we construct around ourselves until we're left facing ourselves, not our roles."[[69]](#cite_note-73) The Shadows represent another [authoritarian](/wiki/Authoritarian "Authoritarian") philosophy cloaked in a disguise of evolution through fire, of fomenting conflict in order to promote evolutionary progress.[[70]](#cite_note-74) Characters affiliated with the Shadows repeatedly ask, "what do you want?" emphasising personal desire and ambition, using it to shape identity,[[68]](#cite_note-signs-72) encouraging conflict between groups who choose to serve their own glory or profit.[[71]](#cite_note-75) The representation of order and chaos was informed by the [Babylonian myth](/wiki/Babylonian_mythology "Babylonian mythology") that the universe was born in the conflict between both. The climax of this conflict comes with the younger races' exposing of the Vorlons' and the Shadows' "true faces"[[66]](#cite_note-js-70) and the rejection of both philosophies,[[68]](#cite_note-signs-72) heralding the dawn of a new age without their interference.

The notion that the war was about "killing your parents"[[66]](#cite_note-js-70) is echoed in the portrayal of the civil war between the human colonies and Earth. Deliberately dealing in historical and political metaphor, with particular emphasis upon [McCarthyism](/wiki/McCarthyism "McCarthyism") and the [HUAC](/wiki/House_Un-American_Activities_Committee "House Un-American Activities Committee"),[[72]](#cite_note-commie-76) the [Earth Alliance](/wiki/Earth_Alliance_(Babylon_5) "Earth Alliance (Babylon 5)") becomes increasingly authoritarian, eventually sliding into a dictatorship. The show examines the impositions on [civil liberties](/wiki/Civil_liberties "Civil liberties") under the pretext of greater defense against outside threats which aid its rise, and the self-delusion of a populace which believes its moral superiority will never allow a dictatorship to come to power, until it is too late.[[73]](#cite_note-77) The successful rebellion led by the Babylon 5 station results in the restoration of a democratic government and true autonomy for Mars and the colonies.[[74]](#cite_note-78)

### War and peace

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: War and peace")]

> What interests me, what I wanted to do with making this show, was in large measure to examine the issues and emotions and events that precede a war, precipitate a war, the effects of the war itself, the end of the war and the aftermath of the war. The war is hardware; the people are at the center of the story.

—J. Michael Straczynski, 1997[[75]](#cite_note-jswar-79)

The *Babylon 5* universe portrays numerous armed conflicts on an interstellar scale, including the [Dilgar war](/wiki/Deathwalker "Deathwalker"), [Narn](/wiki/Narn "Narn")-[Centauri](/wiki/Centauri_(Babylon_5) "Centauri (Babylon 5)") conflict, [Minbari civil war](/wiki/Rumors,_Bargains_and_Lies "Rumors, Bargains and Lies"), [Drakh War](/wiki/Drakh#Centauri "Drakh"), [Interstellar Alliance-Centauri war](/wiki/And_All_My_Dreams,_Torn_Asunder "And All My Dreams, Torn Asunder"), and [the Great Burn.](/wiki/The_Deconstruction_of_Falling_Stars "The Deconstruction of Falling Stars")[[76]](#cite_note-80) The story begins in the aftermath of a war which brought the human race to the brink of extinction, caused by a misunderstanding during a [first contact](/wiki/First_contact_(science_fiction) "First contact (science fiction)") with the Minbari.[[77]](#cite_note-january-81) Babylon 5 is built to foster peace through diplomacy, described as the "last, best hope for peace" in the opening credits monologue during its first three seasons. Wars between separate alien civilizations are featured. The conflict between the [Narn](/wiki/Narn "Narn") and the [Centauri](/wiki/Centauri_(Babylon_5) "Centauri (Babylon 5)") is followed from its beginnings as a minor territorial dispute amplified by historical animosity, through to its end, in which [weapons of mass destruction](/wiki/Weapons_of_mass_destruction "Weapons of mass destruction") are employed to subjugate and enslave a planet. The war is an attempt to portray a more sobering kind of conflict than usually seen on science fiction television. Informed by the events of the first [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War"), the [Cuban Missile Crisis](/wiki/Cuban_Missile_Crisis "Cuban Missile Crisis") and the [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") [invasion of Prague](/wiki/Prague_Spring#Invasion "Prague Spring"), the intent was to recreate these moments when "the world held its breath" and the emotional core of the conflict was the disbelief that the situation could have occurred at all, and the desperation to find a way to bring it to an end.[[78]](#cite_note-82) By the start of the third season, the opening monolog had changed to say that the hope for peace had "failed" and the Babylon 5 station had become the "last, best hope for victory", indicating that while peace is ostensibly a laudable goal, it can also mean a capitulation to an enemy intent on committing horrendous acts and that "peace is a byproduct of victory against those who do not want peace."[[79]](#cite_note-83)

The Shadow War also features prominently in the show, wherein the Shadows work to instigate conflict between other races to promote technological and cultural advancement, opposed by the Vorlons who are attempting to impose their own authoritarian philosophy of obedience. The gradual discovery of the scheme and the rebellion against it underpin the first three seasons,[[80]](#cite_note-84) but also as a wider metaphor for competing forces of order and chaos. In that respect, Straczynski stated he presented Earth's descent into a dictatorship as its own "shadow war".[[81]](#cite_note-home-85) In ending the Shadow War before the conclusion of the series, the show was able to more fully explore its aftermath, and it is this "war at home" which forms the bulk of the remaining two seasons. The struggle for independence between [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") and Earth culminates with a civil war between the human colonies (led by the Babylon 5 station) and the home planet. Choosing Mars as both the spark for the civil war, and the staging ground for its dramatic conclusion, enabled the viewer to understand the conflict more fully than had it involved an anonymous colony orbiting a distant star.[[61]](#cite_note-mars-65) The conflict, and the reasons behind it, were informed by [Nazism](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism"), McCarthyism and the [breakup of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Breakup_of_Yugoslavia "Breakup of Yugoslavia"),[[72]](#cite_note-commie-76) and the destruction of the state also served as partial inspiration for the Minbari civil war.[[82]](#cite_note-86)[[83]](#cite_note-87)

The post-war landscape has its roots in the [Reconstruction](/wiki/Reconstruction_era_of_the_United_States "Reconstruction era of the United States"). The attempt to resolve the [issues of the American Civil War](/wiki/Issues_of_the_American_Civil_War "Issues of the American Civil War") after the conflict had ended, and this struggle for survival in a changed world was also informed by works such as *[Alas, Babylon](/wiki/Alas,_Babylon "Alas, Babylon")*, a novel dealing with the after-effects of a [nuclear war](/wiki/Nuclear_warfare "Nuclear warfare") on a small American town.[[84]](#cite_note-88) The show expresses that the end of these wars is not an end to war itself. Events shown hundreds of years into the show's future tell of wars which will once again bring the human race to the edge of annihilation, demonstrating that humanity will not change, and the best that can be hoped for after it falls is that it climbs a little higher each time, until it can one day "take [its] place among the stars, teaching those who follow."[[85]](#cite_note-89)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Religion")]

> If you look at the long history of human society, religion – whether you describe that as organized, disorganized, or the various degrees of accepted superstition – has always been present. And it will be present 200 years from now... To totally ignore that part of the human equation would be as false and wrong-headed as ignoring the fact that people get mad, or passionate, or strive for better lives.

—J. Michael Straczynski, 1993[[86]](#cite_note-religion1-90)

Many of Earth's contemporary religions are shown to still exist, with the main human characters often having religious convictions. Among those specifically identified are the [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism "Roman Catholicism") branch of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") (including the [Jesuits](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus")), [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), and the fictional Foundationism (which developed after first contact with alien races).[[87]](#cite_note-91) Alien beliefs in the show range from the [Centauri](/wiki/Centauri_(Babylon_5) "Centauri (Babylon 5)")'s [Bacchanalian](/wiki/Bacchanalia "Bacchanalia")-influenced religions,[[86]](#cite_note-religion1-90) of which there are up to seventy different denominations,[[88]](#cite_note-92) to the more [pantheistic](/wiki/Pantheism "Pantheism") as with the [Narn](/wiki/Narn "Narn") and [Minbari](/wiki/Minbari "Minbari") religions.[[89]](#cite_note-93) In the show's third season, a community of [Cistercian monks](/wiki/Cistercians "Cistercians") takes up residence on the Babylon 5 station, in order to learn what other races call God,[[90]](#cite_note-94) and to come to a better understanding of the different religions through study at close quarters.[[91]](#cite_note-95)

References to both human and alien religion is often subtle and brief, but can also form the main theme of an episode.[[92]](#cite_note-96) The first-season episode "[The Parliament of Dreams](/wiki/The_Parliament_of_Dreams "The Parliament of Dreams")" is a conventional "showcase" for religion, in which each species on the Babylon 5 station has an opportunity to demonstrate its beliefs (humanity's are presented as being numerous and varied),[[86]](#cite_note-religion1-90) while "[Passing Through Gethsemane](/wiki/Passing_Through_Gethsemane "Passing Through Gethsemane")" focuses on a specific position of Roman Catholic beliefs,[[93]](#cite_note-97) as well as concepts of justice, vengeance, and biblical forgiveness.[[94]](#cite_note-98) Other treatments have been more contentious, such as the [David Gerrold](/wiki/David_Gerrold "David Gerrold")-scripted "[Believers](/wiki/Believers_(Babylon_5) "Believers (Babylon 5)")", in which alien parents would rather see their son die than undergo a life-saving operation because their religious beliefs forbid it.[[86]](#cite_note-religion1-90)

When religion is an integral part of an episode, various characters express differing view points. In the episode "[Soul Hunter](/wiki/Soul_Hunter_(Babylon_5) "Soul Hunter (Babylon 5)")", where the concept of an [immortal soul](/wiki/Soul_(spirit) "Soul (spirit)") is touched upon, and whether after death it is destroyed, reincarnated, or simply does not exist. The character arguing the latter, [Doctor Stephen Franklin](/wiki/Stephen_Franklin_(Babylon_5) "Stephen Franklin (Babylon 5)"), often appears in the more spiritual storylines as his scientific rationality is used to create dramatic conflict. Undercurrents of religions such as Buddhism have been viewed by some in various episode scripts,[[95]](#cite_note-99) and while identifying himself as an [atheist](/wiki/Atheist "Atheist"),[[86]](#cite_note-religion1-90) Straczynski believes that passages of dialog can take on distinct meanings to viewers of differing faiths, and that the show ultimately expresses ideas which cross religious boundaries.[[96]](#cite_note-100)

### Addiction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Addiction")]

Substance abuse and its impact on human personalities also features in the *Babylon 5* storyline. Garibaldi is a relapsing-remitting alcoholic, who practices complete [abstinence](/wiki/Abstinence "Abstinence") throughout most of the series until the [middle of season five](/wiki/Phoenix_Rising_(Babylon_5) "Phoenix Rising (Babylon 5)"), only recovering at the end of the season. [Zack Allan](/wiki/Zack_Allan "Zack Allan"), his eventual replacement as chief of security, was given a second chance by Garibaldi after overcoming his own addiction to an unspecified drug. Dr. [Stephen Franklin](/wiki/Stephen_Franklin_(Babylon_5) "Stephen Franklin (Babylon 5)") develops an addiction to injectable [stimulant](/wiki/Stimulant "Stimulant") drugs while trying to cope with the chronic stress and work overload in Medlab, and he takes a leave of absence from his position to recover. Executive Officer [Susan Ivanova](/wiki/Susan_Ivanova "Susan Ivanova") mentions that her father became an alcoholic after her mother's suicide. Captain [Elizabeth Lochley](/wiki/Elizabeth_Lochley "Elizabeth Lochley") tells Garibaldi that her father was an alcoholic, and that she is a recovering alcoholic herself.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Influences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Influences")]

*Babylon 5* draws upon a number of cultural, historical, political and religious influences to inform and illustrate its characters and storylines. Straczynski has stated that there was no intent to wholly represent any particular period of history or preceding work of fiction, but has acknowledged their influence on the series, inasmuch as it uses similar well established storytelling structures, such as the [hero's journey](/wiki/Hero%27s_journey "Hero's journey").[[97]](#cite_note-101)[[98]](#cite_note-102)

There are a number of specific literary references. Several episodes take their titles from Shakespearean monologs,[[99]](#cite_note-103)[[100]](#cite_note-104) and at least one character quotes Shakespeare directly.[[101]](#cite_note-105) The Psi-Cop [Alfred Bester](/wiki/Alfred_Bester_(Babylon_5) "Alfred Bester (Babylon 5)") was named after the [science fiction author of the same name](/wiki/Alfred_Bester "Alfred Bester"), as his work influenced the autocratic [Psi Corps](/wiki/Psi_Corps "Psi Corps") organization the character represents.[[102]](#cite_note-106)

There are a number of references to the [legend of King Arthur](/wiki/Legend_of_King_Arthur "Legend of King Arthur"), with ships named *Excalibur* appearing in the main series and the [*Crusade*](/wiki/Crusade_(TV_series) "Crusade (TV series)") spin-off, and a character in "[A Late Delivery from Avalon](/wiki/A_Late_Delivery_from_Avalon "A Late Delivery from Avalon")" claiming to possess the [sword](/wiki/Excalibur "Excalibur") itself. Straczynski links the incident which sparked the Earth–Minbari war, in which actions are misinterpreted during a tense situation, to a sequence in *[Le Morte d'Arthur](/wiki/Le_Morte_d%27Arthur "Le Morte d'Arthur")*, in which a standoff between two armies turns violent when innocent actions are misinterpreted as hostile.[[103]](#cite_note-107)

The series also quotes contemporary and ancient history. The [Centauri](/wiki/Centauri_(Babylon_5) "Centauri (Babylon 5)") are in part modeled on the Roman empire.[[104]](#cite_note-108) Emperor [Cartagia](/wiki/Cartagia "Cartagia") believes himself to be a god, a deliberate reference to [Caligula](/wiki/Caligula "Caligula").[[105]](#cite_note-Killick-109) His eventual assassination leads to the ascension of Londo and eventually Vir, both unlikely candidates for the throne, similar to [Claudius](/wiki/Claudius "Claudius")' improbable ascension after Caligula was assassinated.[[106]](#cite_note-110) The series alludes to the novel *[I, Claudius](/wiki/I,_Claudius "I, Claudius")* by [Robert Graves](/wiki/Robert_Graves "Robert Graves") when Cartagia jokes that he has cured a man of his cough by having him beheaded, something also done by Caligula.[[107]](#cite_note-111) In the episode "[In the Shadow of Z'ha'dhum](/wiki/In_the_Shadow_of_Z%27ha%27dum "In the Shadow of Z'ha'dum")," Sheridan ponders [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill")'s [Coventry dilemma](/wiki/Coventry_Blitz#Coventry_and_Ultra "Coventry Blitz"), of whether or not to act on covertly gathered intelligence during a war. Lives would be saved, but at the risk of revealing to the enemy that their intentions are known, which may be far more damaging in the long term. The swearing in of Vice President Morgan Clark invokes the [assassination of President John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_assassination "John F. Kennedy assassination"), being deliberately staged to mirror the scene aboard [Air Force One](/wiki/Air_Force_One "Air Force One") when [Lyndon Johnson was sworn in as President](/wiki/First_inauguration_of_Lyndon_B._Johnson "First inauguration of Lyndon B. Johnson").[[108]](#cite_note-112)

Several episodes have titles referring to Biblical and/or Christian traditional narratives or texts, such as "[Passing Through Gethsemane](/wiki/Passing_Through_Gethsemane "Passing Through Gethsemane")", "[A Voice in the Wilderness](/wiki/A_Voice_in_the_Wilderness_(Babylon_5) "A Voice in the Wilderness (Babylon 5)")," and "[And the Rock Cried Out, No Hiding Place](/wiki/And_the_Rock_Cried_Out,_No_Hiding_Place "And the Rock Cried Out, No Hiding Place")," the latter being a line from the [gospel song](/wiki/Gospel_music "Gospel music") "There's no Hiding Place Down Here."

## Use of the Internet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Use of the Internet")]

Main article: [Babylon 5's use of the Internet](/wiki/Babylon_5%27s_use_of_the_Internet "Babylon 5's use of the Internet")

The show employed Internet marketing to create a buzz among online readers far in advance of the airing of the pilot episode,[[109]](#cite_note-113) with Straczynski participating in online communities on [USENET](/wiki/USENET "USENET") (in the [rec.arts.sf.tv.babylon5.moderated](/wiki/Rec.arts.sf.tv.babylon5.moderated "Rec.arts.sf.tv.babylon5.moderated") newsgroup), and the [GEnie](/wiki/GEnie "GEnie") and [CompuServe](/wiki/CompuServe "CompuServe") systems before the Web came together as it exists today. The station's location, in "grid epsilon" at coordinates of 470/18/22, was a reference to [GEnie](/wiki/GEnie "GEnie") ("grid epsilon" = "GE")[[110]](#cite_note-114) and the original forum's address on the system's bulletin boards (page 470, category 18, topic 22).[[111]](#cite_note-115)

Also during this time, [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures") executive Jim Moloshok created and distributed electronic [trading cards](/wiki/Trading_cards "Trading cards") to help advertise the series.[[112]](#cite_note-116) In 1995, Warner Bros. started the Official *Babylon 5* Website on the now defunct [Pathfinder](/wiki/Pathfinder_(website) "Pathfinder (website)") portal. In September 1995, they hired series fan Troy Rutter to take over the site and move it to its own domain name, and to oversee the *Keyword B5* area on [America Online](/wiki/AOL "AOL").[[113]](#cite_note-117)

### *Star Trek: Deep Space Nine* and Paramount plagiarism controversy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Star Trek: Deep Space Nine and Paramount plagiarism controversy")]

Straczynski indicated that [Paramount Television](/wiki/Paramount_Television "Paramount Television") was aware of his concept as early as 1989,[[121]](#cite_note-125)[[122]](#cite_note-126) when he attempted to sell the show to the studio, and provided them with the series [bible](/wiki/Bible_(writing) "Bible (writing)"), pilot script, artwork, lengthy character background histories, and plot synopses for "22 or so" planned episodes taken from the overall course of the planned series.[[123]](#cite_note-127)[[124]](#cite_note-128)

Paramount declined to produce *Babylon 5*, but later announced *[Star Trek: Deep Space Nine](/wiki/Star_Trek:_Deep_Space_Nine "Star Trek: Deep Space Nine")* was in development, two months after Warner Bros. announced its plans for *Babylon 5*. Unlike previous *Star Trek* shows, *Deep Space Nine* was based on a space station, and had themes similar to those of *Babylon 5*, which drew some to compare it with *Babylon 5*. Straczynski stated that, even though he was confident that *Deep Space Nine* showrunners [Rick Berman](/wiki/Rick_Berman "Rick Berman") and [Michael Piller](/wiki/Michael_Piller "Michael Piller") had not seen the material, he suspected that Paramount executives used his bible and scripts to steer development of *Deep Space Nine*.[[125]](#cite_note-129)[[126]](#cite_note-130)[[127]](#cite_note-GEnie-131) Straczynski and Warner did not file suit against Paramount, largely because he believed it would negatively affect both series. He argued the same when confronted by claims that the lack of legal action was proof that his allegation was unfounded.[[127]](#cite_note-GEnie-131) According to a 2017 interview with Patricia Tallman, there was a legal case and an out-of-court settlement with Paramount.[[128]](#cite_note-132)

### Influence and legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Influence and legacy")]

Generally viewed as having "launched the new era of television CGI visual effects",[[129]](#cite_note-133) *Babylon 5* received multiple awards during its initial run, including two consecutive Hugo Awards for [best dramatic presentation](/wiki/Hugo_Award_for_Best_Dramatic_Presentation "Hugo Award for Best Dramatic Presentation"),[[117]](#cite_note-Hugo1-121)[[118]](#cite_note-Hugo2-122) and continues to feature in polls and listings of top-rated science fiction series.[[130]](#cite_note-134)[[131]](#cite_note-135)[[132]](#cite_note-136)

*Babylon 5* has been praised for its depth and complexity against a backdrop of contemporary shows which largely lacked long-term consequences, with plots typically being resolved in the course of an episode, occasionally two.[[133]](#cite_note-137) Straczynski wrote 92 of 110 teleplays, a greater proportion than some of his contemporaries.[[134]](#cite_note-138) Reviewers rated the quality of writing on a widely varying scale, identifying both eloquent [soliloquies](/wiki/Soliloquy "Soliloquy") and dialogue that felt "stilted and theatrical."[[135]](#cite_note-:0-139)[[136]](#cite_note-:1-140)

Straczynski has claimed that the multi-year story arc, now a feature of most mainstream televised drama, is the legacy of the series.[[137]](#cite_note-syfy_oral-141) He stated that [Ronald D. Moore](/wiki/Ronald_D._Moore "Ronald D. Moore") and [Damon Lindelof](/wiki/Damon_Lindelof "Damon Lindelof") used the 5-year narrative structure of *Babylon 5* as blueprints for their respective shows, *[Battlestar Galactica](/wiki/Battlestar_Galactica_(2004_TV_series) "Battlestar Galactica (2004 TV series)")* and *[Lost](/wiki/Lost_(TV_series) "Lost (TV series)")*.[[20]](#cite_note-newsarama_2019-24) He also claims *Babylon 5* was the first series to be shot in the [16:9 aspect ratio](/wiki/16:9_aspect_ratio "16:9 aspect ratio"), and to use 5.1 channel sound mixes.[[137]](#cite_note-syfy_oral-141) It was an early example of widespread use of CGI rather than models for space scenes, which allowed for more freedom and larger scale in creating the scenes. While praised at the time, due to budgetary and mastering issues these sequences are considered to have aged poorly.[[135]](#cite_note-:0-139)[[138]](#cite_note-142)

A theme among reviewers is that the series was more than the sum of its parts: while criticizing the writing, directing, acting and effects, particularly by comparison to current television productions, reviewers praised the consistency of plotting over the series' run, transcending the quality of its individual elements. Many retrospectives, while criticizing virtually every aspect of the production, have praised the series for its narrative cohesion and contribution to serialized television.[[136]](#cite_note-:1-140)[[139]](#cite_note-143)[[140]](#cite_note-144)[[141]](#cite_note-145)[[142]](#cite_note-146) Producer John Copeland said: "You're not really tuning in to watch the visual effects," instead, people are watching a two-decades-old show because "the storytelling does hold up" and "you wanna spend more time with the characters."[[143]](#cite_note-Cooper-147)

[DC](/wiki/DC_Comics "DC Comics") began publishing [*Babylon 5* comics](/wiki/Babylon_5_novels,_short_stories_and_comic_books#Comic_books "Babylon 5 novels, short stories and comic books") in 1994, with stories (initially written by Straczynski) that closely tied in with events depicted in the show, with events in the comics eventually being referenced onscreen in the television series.[[144]](#cite_note-148) The [franchise](/wiki/Media_franchise#Development_to_other_forms "Media franchise") continued to expand into short stories, RPGs, and novels, with the [Technomage trilogy](/wiki/Technomage_(Babylon_5) "Technomage (Babylon 5)") of books being the last to be published in 2001, shortly after the spin-off television series, *[Crusade](/wiki/Crusade_(TV_series) "Crusade (TV series)")*, was canceled. Excepting movie rights, which are retained by Straczynski, all production rights for the franchise are owned by Warner Bros.[[145]](#cite_note-149)

## Media franchise

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Media franchise")]

Main article: [Babylon 5 (franchise)](/wiki/Babylon_5_(franchise) "Babylon 5 (franchise)")

In November 1994, [DC](/wiki/DC_Comics "DC Comics") began publishing monthly *Babylon 5* comics. A number of short stories and novels were also produced between 1995 and 2001. Additional books were published by the gaming companies Chameleon Eclectic and [Mongoose Publishing](/wiki/Mongoose_Publishing "Mongoose Publishing"), to support their desk-top strategy and [role-playing](/wiki/Role-playing_game "Role-playing game") games.

Three TV films were released by [TNT](/wiki/TNT_(American_TV_network) "TNT (American TV network)") in 1998, after funding a fifth season of *Babylon 5*, following the demise of the [Prime Time Entertainment Network](/wiki/Prime_Time_Entertainment_Network "Prime Time Entertainment Network") the previous year. In addition to *[In the Beginning](/wiki/Babylon_5:_In_the_Beginning "Babylon 5: In the Beginning")*, *[Thirdspace](/wiki/Babylon_5:_Thirdspace "Babylon 5: Thirdspace")*, and *[The River of Souls](/wiki/Babylon_5:_The_River_of_Souls "Babylon 5: The River of Souls")*, they released a re-edited special edition of the original 1993 TV film, *[The Gathering](/wiki/Babylon_5:_The_Gathering "Babylon 5: The Gathering")*. In 1999, a fifth TV film was also produced, *[A Call to Arms](/wiki/Babylon_5:_A_Call_to_Arms "Babylon 5: A Call to Arms")*, which acted as a pilot movie for the spin-off series *[Crusade](/wiki/Crusade_(TV_series) "Crusade (TV series)")*, which TNT canceled after 13 episodes had been filmed.

[Dell Publishing](/wiki/Dell_Publishing "Dell Publishing") started publication of a series of *Babylon 5* novels in 1995, which were ostensibly considered canon within the TV series' continuity, nominally supervised by Straczynski, with later novels in the line being more directly based upon Straczynski's own notes and story outlines. In 1997, [Del Rey](/wiki/Del_Rey_Books "Del Rey Books") obtained the publication license from Warner Bros., and proceeded to release a number of original trilogies directly scenarized by Straczynski, as well as novelizations of three of the TNT telefilms (*In the Beginning, Thirdspace*, and *A Call to Arms*). All of the Del Rey novels are considered completely canonical within the filmic *Babylon 5* universe.

In 2000, the [Sci-Fi Channel](/wiki/Syfy "Syfy") purchased the rights to rerun the *Babylon 5* series, and it premiered a new telefilm, *[The Legend of the Rangers](/wiki/Babylon_5:_The_Legend_of_the_Rangers "Babylon 5: The Legend of the Rangers")* in 2002, which failed to be picked up as a series. In 2007, the first in a planned anthology of straight-to-DVD short stories, entitled *[The Lost Tales](/wiki/Babylon_5:_The_Lost_Tales "Babylon 5: The Lost Tales")*, was released by [Warner Home Video](/wiki/Warner_Home_Video "Warner Home Video"), but no others were produced, due to funding issues.[[146]](#cite_note-150)

Straczynski announced a *Babylon 5* film at the 2014 [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con"),[[147]](#cite_note-151) but stated in 2016 that it had been delayed while he completed other productions.[[148]](#cite_note-152) In 2018, Straczynski stated that although he possesses the movie rights, he believed that neither a film nor television series revival would happen while Warner Bros. retained the intellectual property for the TV series, believing that Warner Bros would insist on handling production, and that other studios would be hesitant to produce a film without also having the rights to the TV series.[[149]](#cite_note-153)

### Reboot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Reboot")]

A reboot of *Babylon 5* was announced in September 2021. It was to be produced by Straczynski through [Studio JMS](/wiki/Studio_JMS "Studio JMS"), and developed by Warner Bros. Television for [The CW](/wiki/The_CW "The CW"),[[150]](#cite_note-154) before Warner took the rights back.[[151]](#cite_note-155)

### Direct-to-video animated film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Direct-to-video animated film")]

Main article: [Babylon 5: The Road Home](/wiki/Babylon_5:_The_Road_Home "Babylon 5: The Road Home")

Straczynski revealed in May 2023 that he had been working in secret with [Warner Bros. Animation](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Animation "Warner Bros. Animation") to produce *[Babylon 5: The Road Home](/wiki/Babylon_5:_The_Road_Home "Babylon 5: The Road Home")*, a full-length animated film.[[152]](#cite_note-156)[[153]](#cite_note-157) Most of the main surviving cast members voice their characters in the film, including Boxleitner, Christian, Jurasik, Mumy, Scoggins and Tallman. Other voice actors, replacing roles of those that had died, including Paul Guyet as Zathras and Jeffery Sinclair, Anthony Hansen as Michael Garibaldi, Mara Junot as Reporter and Computer Voice, [Phil LaMarr](/wiki/Phil_LaMarr "Phil LaMarr") as Dr. Stephen Franklin, Piotr Michael as David Sheridan, Andrew Morgado as G'Kar, and Rebecca Riedy as Delenn.[[154]](#cite_note-158) The film premiered at [Comic-Con](/wiki/Comic-Con "Comic-Con") in July 2023, and was made available on streaming services and physical media on August 15, 2023.[[155]](#cite_note-159)

## Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Home media")]

In July 1995, [Warner Home Video](/wiki/Warner_Home_Video "Warner Home Video") began distributing *Babylon 5* VHS video tapes under its Beyond Vision label in the UK. Beginning with the original telefilm, *[The Gathering](/wiki/Babylon_5:_The_Gathering "Babylon 5: The Gathering")*, these were [PAL](/wiki/PAL "PAL") tapes, showing video in the same [4:3 aspect ratio](/wiki/Aspect_ratio_(image)#Current_video_standards "Aspect ratio (image)") as the initial television broadcasts. By the release of Season 2, tapes included [closed captioning](/wiki/Closed_captioning "Closed captioning") of dialogue and [Dolby Surround](/wiki/Dolby_Surround "Dolby Surround") sound. [Columbia House](/wiki/Columbia_House "Columbia House") began distributing [NTSC](/wiki/NTSC "NTSC") tapes, via mail order in 1997, followed by repackaged collector's editions and three-tape [boxed sets](/wiki/Box_sets "Box sets") in 1999, by which time the original pilot telefilm had been replaced by the re-edited TNT special edition. Additional movie and complete season boxed-sets were also released by Warner Bros. until 2000.

[Image Entertainment](/wiki/Image_Entertainment "Image Entertainment") released *Babylon 5* [laserdiscs](/wiki/Laserdiscs "Laserdiscs") between December 1998 and September 1999. Produced on double-sided 12-inch [Pioneer](/wiki/Pioneer_Corporation "Pioneer Corporation") discs, each contained two episodes displayed in the 4:3 broadcast aspect-ratio, with Dolby Surround audio and closed captioning for the dialogue. Starting with two TNT telefilms, *[In the Beginning](/wiki/Babylon_5:_In_the_Beginning "Babylon 5: In the Beginning")* and the re-edited special edition of *The Gathering*, Seasons 1 and 5 were released simultaneously over a six-month period. Seasons 2 and 4 followed, but with the decision to halt production due to a drop in sales, precipitated by rumors of a pending DVD release, only the first twelve episodes of Season 2 and the first six episodes of Season 4 were ultimately released.[[156]](#cite_note-160)

In November 2001, Warner Home Video began distributing *Babylon 5* DVDs with a two-movie set containing the re-edited TNT special edition of *The Gathering* and *In The Beginning*. The telefilms were later individually released in [region 2](/wiki/DVD_region_code#Region_codes_and_countries "DVD region code") in April 2002, though some markets received the original version of *The Gathering* in identical packaging.

DVD boxed sets of the individual seasons, each containing six discs, began being released in October 2002. Each included a printed booklet containing episode summaries, with each disc containing audio options for German, French, and English, plus [subtitles](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Special:Search/subtitle "wikt:Special:Search/subtitle") in a wider range of languages, including Arabic and Dutch. Video was digitally remastered from higher resolution PAL broadcast masters and displayed in [anamorphic widescreen](/wiki/Anamorphic_widescreen "Anamorphic widescreen") with remastered and remixed [Dolby Digital](/wiki/Dolby_Digital "Dolby Digital") [5.1](/wiki/5.1_surround_sound "5.1 surround sound") sound. Disc 1 of each set contained an introduction to the season by [Straczynski](/wiki/J._Michael_Straczynski "J. Michael Straczynski"), while disc 6 included [featurettes](/wiki/Featurettes "Featurettes") containing interviews with various production staff, as well as information on the [fictional universe](/wiki/Fictional_universe "Fictional universe"), and a [gag reel](/wiki/Gag_reel "Gag reel"). Three episodes in each season also contained commentary from either Straczynski, members of the main cast, and/or the episode director.

Since its initial release, a number of repackaged DVD boxed sets have been produced for various [regional markets](/wiki/DVD_region_code#Region_codes_and_countries "DVD region code"). With slightly altered [cover art](/wiki/Cover_art "Cover art"), they included no additional content, but the discs were more securely stored in slimline cases, rather than the early "book" format, with hard plastic pages used during the original release of the first three seasons.

### Mastering problems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Babylon_5&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Mastering problems")]

While the series was in pre-production, studios were looking at ways for their existing shows to make the transition from the standard [4:3 aspect ratio](/wiki/Aspect_ratio_(image)#4:3_standard "Aspect ratio (image)") to the [widescreen](/wiki/Widescreen "Widescreen") formats that would accompany the next generation of televisions. After visiting [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures"), which was stretching the [horizontal interval](/wiki/Horizontal_blanking_interval "Horizontal blanking interval") for an episode of *[Lois & Clark](/wiki/Lois_%26_Clark:_The_New_Adventures_of_Superman "Lois & Clark: The New Adventures of Superman")*, producer John Copeland convinced them to allow *Babylon 5* to be shot on [Super 35mm film stock](/wiki/Super_35 "Super 35"). "The idea being that we would [telecine](/wiki/Telecine "Telecine") to 4:3 for the original broadcast of the series. But what it also gave us was a negative that had been shot for the new 16×9 widescreen-format televisions that we knew were on the horizon."[[158]](#cite_note-Copeland74-162)

> The widescreen conversion thing was executive short sightedness at its finest!!! We offered to do ALL of *Babylon 5* in widescreen mode if Warner Bros would buy us a reference monitor so we could check our output (only $5000 at the time). Ken Parkes (the "Business affairs" guy) and Netter (penny wise, but pound foolish) said no! So we did everything so it could be CROPPED to be widescreen! Each blamed the other by the way. Doug Netter said, "Ken Parkes said no". Ken Parkes said, "Doug Netter said no". SHEESH!!! So for $75 an episode they could have had AWESOME near Hi-Def.

— Ron Thornton, 2008[[159]](#cite_note-163)

Though the CG scenes, and those containing live action combined with digital elements, could have been created in a suitable widescreen format, a cost-saving decision was taken to produce them in the 4:3 aspect ratio. When those images were prepared for widescreen release, the top and bottom of the images were simply cropped, and the remaining image 'blown up' to match the dimensions of the live action footage, noticeably reducing the image quality.[[158]](#cite_note-Copeland74-162)[[143]](#cite_note-Cooper-147) The scenes containing live action ready to be [composited](/wiki/Digital_compositing "Digital compositing") with [matte paintings](/wiki/Matte_paintings "Matte paintings"), [CG animation](/wiki/Computer_animation "Computer animation"), etc., were delivered on tape already telecined to the 4:3 aspect-ratio, and contained a high level of [grain](/wiki/Film_grain "Film grain"), which resulted in further [image noise](/wiki/Image_noise "Image noise") being present when enlarged and stretched for widescreen.[[160]](#cite_note-164)

For the purely live-action scenes, rather than using the film negatives, according to Copeland, "Warners had even forgotten that they had those. They used [PAL](/wiki/PAL "PAL") versions and converted them to [NTSC](/wiki/NTSC "NTSC") for the US market. They actually didn't go back and retransfer the shows."[[161]](#cite_note-Copeland75-165) With the resulting [aliasing](/wiki/Aliasing "Aliasing"), and the [progressive scan](/wiki/Progressive_scan "Progressive scan") transfer of the video to DVD, this has created a number of visual flaws throughout the widescreen release. In particular, quality has been noted to drop significantly in composite shots.[[162]](#cite_note-166)[[163]](#cite_note-167)

In 2020, a new remastered version was created (as detailed above)[[164]](#cite_note-168) which restored the original picture quality and repaired the damage to the CGI scenes, although this also involved reverting to the original 4:3 aspect ratio. The negatives were scanned at ultra HD quality and then down converted to HD and upscaling programs were used to enhance the CGI to HD at the same time.
